coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of levofloxacin and copper (II) based on fluorescence “turn on-off” of biomass carbonized polymer dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Jiasheng
Yang, Shuliang
Wang, Yanjie
Cui, Yahan
Li, Qiaoyan
Chen, Yanhua
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: A novel type of biomass carbonized polymer dots (CS-CPDs) with green fluorescence emission were synthesized via a one-step microwave-assisted method, using chitosan and maleic acid as the precursors. The fluorescence intensity of CS-CPDs was selectively enhanced (turn on) with increasing levofloxacin (LEV) concentration based on Aggregation Induced Emission Enhancement. The linear range of this method was 1–35 μmol/L, with the limit of detection of 0.20 μmol/L. Furthermore, we demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of CS-CPDs/LEV was restored (turn on-off) due to the addition of copper (II), which could assemble a more stable Cu2+/LEV complex through coordination. For the first time, CS-CPDs were utilized as a selective fluorescent “turn on-off” probe for the continuous detection of LEV and Cu2+. Excellent results were also obtained in environmental water samples analysis with recoveries ranging of 91.3–99.6% and 97.7–113.4%, respectively. Moreover, the prepared CS-CPDs exhibited excellent optical stability and large Stokes shift (139 nm), which predicted broad application prospects in the field of fluorescence sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Chitosan
Fluorescent “turn on-off” detection
Levofloxacin
Copper (II)
Aggregation induced emission enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of LEV and Cu2+
Fluorescence sensitivity and selectivity towards levofloxacin
Fluorescence “turn on-off” detection of Cu2+ and the investigation on selectivity
Detection of LEV and Cu2+ in environmental water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CS-CPDs (a); XRD spectrum of CS-CPDs (b) and FT-IR spectra of CS-CPDs and chitosan (c).
XPS survey spectrum (a) and high-resolution spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) of the CS-CPDs.

UV–vis absorption (black line), excitation (blue line) and emission spectra (red line) of CS-CPDs in aqueous solution (a); fluorescence spectra of CS-CPDs excited at different wavelengths in the range of 330–370 nm (b). Inset: fluorescence spectra of CS-CPDs excited at different wavelengths in the range of 380–440 nm.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CS-CPDs with addition of indicated concentrations of LEV (a); the linear relationship between F/F0 and CLEV (b); The chemical structures of Levofloxacin, Ofloxacin, Norfloxacin, Ciprofloxacin and Enrofloxacin (c); Response of fluorescence signal (F/F0 ) of CS-CPDs for LEV, OFL, NOR, CIP and ENO (d).

UV-vis absorption spectra (a) and FT-IR spectra of CS-CPDs, LEV, CS-CPDs/LEV and CS-CPDs/LEV/Cu2+ (b); the Zeta potentials of CS-CPDs, LEV, CS-CPDs/LEV, Cu2+ and CS-CPDs/LEV/Cu2+ (c) and the hydrated particle sizes of CS-CPDs and CS-CPDs/LEV (d).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of CS-CPDs and the sensing process of levofloxacin and copper (II).
Applications of CS-CPDs in LEV determination in environmental water samples.
Applications of CS-CPDs/LEV in environmental water samples for the determination of Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of levofloxacin and copper (II) based on fluorescence “turn on-off” of biomass carbonized polymer dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin Province Research Center for Engineering and Technology of Spectral Analytical Instruments, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Province Research Center for Engineering and Technology of Spectral Analytical Instruments

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin Province Research Center for Engineering and Technology of Spectral Analytical Instruments, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China (Y. Chen).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent “turn on-off” probe developed in this paper realized the continuous detection of levofloxacin and copper (II) based on the Aggregation Induced Emission Enhancement (AIEE) and coordination.

A novel type of biomass carbonized polymer dots (CS-CPDs) with green fluorescence emission were synthesized via a one-step microwave-assisted method, using chitosan and maleic acid as the precursors. The fluorescence intensity of CS-CPDs was selectively enhanced (turn on) with increasing levofloxacin (LEV) concentration based on Aggregation Induced Emission Enhancement. The linear range of this method was 1–35 μmol/L, with the limit of detection of 0.20 μmol/L. Furthermore, we demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of CS-CPDs/LEV was restored (turn on-off) due to the addition of copper (II), which could assemble a more stable Cu2+/LEV complex through coordination. For the first time, CS-CPDs were utilized as a selective fluorescent “turn on-off” probe for the continuous detection of LEV and Cu2+. Excellent results were also obtained in environmental water samples analysis with recoveries ranging of 91.3–99.6% and 97.7–113.4%, respectively. Moreover, the prepared CS-CPDs exhibited excellent optical stability and large Stokes shift (139 nm), which predicted broad application prospects in the field of fluorescence sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Levofloxacin (LEV) is a third-generation broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone antibacterial agent. By inhibiting bacterial DNA cyclase, it has good antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [1]. LEV is widely used to treat soft tissue infections, intestinal infections, skin and respiratory infections [2,3]. The vast majority of LEV is excreted in urine and remains in ambient water. Therefore, the misusing of LEV will eventually enter the human body through the environment and food, causing great harm to the human body [4]. It is reported that excessive intake of LEV may cause serious adverse symptoms, such as anaphylactic shock, gastrointestinal reactions, central nervous system damage, and mental disorders [5,6]. In addition, Cu2+ is an essential trace element in the human body and plays an important role in biological physiological processes [7]. Cu2+ enters the human body mainly through drinking water. According to the guidelines of Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the admissible level of Cu2+ in drinking water is ∼20 μM [8]. And the accumulation of Cu2+ may disturb the intracellular balance and cause a variety of human diseases, such as Wilson's disease, prion disease, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease [9–11], etc. Therefore, the development of a simple, rapid and sensitive LEV and Cu2+ detection method is of great significance to environmental safety and human health.

At present, some methods for the detection of LEV and Cu2+ have been reported in the literatures. The main methods of LEV detection include high performance liquid chromatography [12], nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy [1], ultraviolet spectrophotometry [13], electrochemical method [14], enzyme-linked immunosorbent method [15], capillary electrophoresis [16], and Surface-Enhanced Raman spectroscopy [17]. The main methods for detecting Cu2+ include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [18] and atomic absorption spectrometry [19]. Although these methods have high sensitivity and accuracy, most of them are cumbersome to operate, time-consuming, and required expensive instruments and professional operators, which limit their wide application. Fluorescence sensing has attracted more and more attention due to its advantages such as fast response speed, convenient detection, simple operation and high sensitivity [20]. The aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) phenomenon was first reported by Tang's group in 2001 [21]. Up to now, researchers have reported AIEE-based fluorescence sensors [22–24]. AIEE is due to the aggregation phenomenon restricting intramolecular motion, and the energy generates fluorescence enhancement in the form of radiative transitions. Compared with “turn-off” probes, fluorescence “turn-on” probes can reduce false positive signals and dark background.

As a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in various fields [25–30] in recent years due to the excellent physical, chemical and optical properties [31,32]. At present, most CDs are prepared from bottom to top by hydrothermal method, which is simple, economical, homogeneous and environmentally friendly, but usually requires higher reaction temperature and longer reaction time [33]. The microwave-assisted method is a molecular-level heating method that provides strong and uniform heat for the reaction system by directly heating the target molecules through the interaction of microwaves with the raw material molecules [34]. Compared with the traditional hydrothermal method, the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method has the advantages of short heat transfer time, fast reaction rate, low cost and high thermal energy utilization [35], which exhibits a broad application prospect in the efficient synthesis of nanomaterials.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), as a very important branch of CDs, are used in optical sensing due to their excellent fluorescence performance, the flexibility of surface modification, high optical and chemical stability, resistance to photobleaching, and low toxicity [36]. CPDs are formed by the chain or hyperbranched organic polymers through certain crosslinked carbonization under high-temperature hydrothermal condition [37]. Zhao et al. used polyvinyl alcohol and ethylene diamine to prepare polymer dots with blue fluorescence emission, which successfully used as the fluorescent probe for the fluorescence detection of cobalt (II) and manganese (II) in environmental water samples [38]. Liu et al. prepared red emissive nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with optimal emission at around 630 nm by hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine as the precursor, and the as-prepared CPDs are successfully used as a fluorescent probe for bioimaging in vitro and vivo [39]. The precursors of chain-like or hyperbranched organic polymers are usually toxic [29], so it makes sense to choose biomass materials for CPDs synthesis from the perspective of material availability and environmental protection.

In this paper, biomass carbonized polymer dots (CS-CPDs) with green fluorescence emission was prepared via microwave-assisted method at 140 °C for 2 h using the biomass polymer chitosan and maleic acid as the precursors, and the prepared CS-CPDs exhibited excellent optical stability and large Stokes shift (139 nm), with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 17.4%. The fluorescence intensity of CS-CPDs was selectively enhanced with the addition of LEV based on AIEE, and then gradually restored with the continuous addition of Cu2+ due to the formation of stable Cu2+/LEV complex through coordination. The prepared CS-CPDs showed excellent selectivity for LEV, and can even distinguish the dextrorotatory isomer of LEV. Therefore, a new type of fluorescent “turn on-off” probe had been developed for the continuous detection of LEV and Cu2+, and the practicability of the method was verified by the detection of the target in three environmental water samples. This work provided a new idea for the continuous detection of pollutants in environmental water samples.
Chemicals and instruments information can be found in Supporting Information.

Maleic acid (140 mg, 1.2 mmol) and chitosan (168 mg, 1.0 mmol) were dissolved in 14 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min to form a transparent solution. The solution was added to a high-pressure microwave vessel, placed in a microwave digestion instrument and heated at 140 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting brown-yellow solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove insoluble materials. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane and then dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (MWCO, 1000 Da) for 24 h. The filtrate was collected and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The fluorescence QY of CS-CPDs was calculated by using the established methods [40]. Fluorescein in 0.1 mol/L NaOH (QY = 92.5%) was chosen as the standard reference sample to calculate the QY of CS-CPDs. The fluorescence QY of CS-CPDs was calculated by the subsequent equation:
ϕ x
=
ϕ s
∙ I
x
/
I s
∙ A
s
/
A x
∙
(
η x
/
η s
)
2
where ϕ represents QY, I represents the integrated area of the fluorescence emission peak, A stands for the absorbance value, η represents the refractive index of the solvent, and the subscripts x and s represent CS-CPDs and standard sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of LEV and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence detection was performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 1 cm path-length quartz cuvette. 100 μL CS-CPDs, 100 μL acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.1 mol/L) and a certain amount of LEV solution (500 μmol/L) were added into a set of 4 mL centrifuge tubes. The above solution was diluted to a constant volume (2 mL) with deionized water and equilibrated for 3 min. The scanning wavelength range was 391–720 nm with an excitation wavelength of 371 nm, and the emission peak at 510 nm was recorded to evaluate the concentration of LEV. The other parameters were set as follows: the excitation and emission slit widths were set to 2.5 nm and 5 nm, respectively. The response time was 0.4 s and the excitation voltage was 700 V. Each spectrum was obtained after three parallel scans.

In order to achieve Cu2+ detection, 100 μL CS-CPDs, 100 μL acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.1 μmol/L), 100 μL LEV solution (500 μmol/L) and a certain amount of Cu2+ solution (500 μmol/L) were added to a set of 4 mL centrifuge tubes. The above solution was diluted with ultrapure water to a constant volume (2 mL) and equilibrated for 3 min. Fluorescence detection was performed by the above-mentioned method.
The selectivity of the system was investigated by adding other species into the reaction system under the same conditions. All measurements were conducted in triplicate.

Concentrations of LEV and Cu2+ in different environmental water samples were measured to evaluate the practicability of the established method. Lake water samples were collected from South Lake and Yan Lake (Changchun, China), and tap water samples came from our laboratory (Changchun, China). Before fluorescence detection, the environmental water samples were centrifuged for 10 min to remove solid particulates, and then were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter.

Different environmental water samples (South Lake water, Yan Lake water and tap water) were spiked with 5–25 μmol/L LEV (final concentration) or 20–80 μmol/L Cu2+ (final concentration). The spiked water samples were subjected to fluorescence measurement according to the above method and the concentrations of LEV and Cu2+ were calculated according to the standard curve.

The Scheme 1 shows the preparation of CS-CPDs and the sensing process of LEV and Cu2+. In this study, biomass material chitosan and maleic acid were used as the precursors, and the amino and hydroxyl groups of chitosan were crosslinked and carbonized with the carboxyl group of maleic acid under the low-temperature microwave-assisted method to form CS-CPDs for continuous detection of LEV and Cu2+. The preparation conditions were optimized from the concentration of chitosan, reaction time and reaction temperature according to the change of QY (Fig. S1). Firstly, the amount of maleic acid was fixed at 140 mg, and the optimal dosage of the precursor was optimized by changing the concentration of chitosan (10–16 mg/mL). As shown in Fig. S1a, the optimal concentration of chitosan was 12 mg/mL using QY as an evaluation indicator. The effects of reaction time and temperature on the QY of CS-CPDs were investigated and shown in Fig. S1b, S1c, and the optimum preparation conditions were reaction time 2 h and reaction temperature 140 ℃. Finally, 168 mg chitosan, 140 mg maleic acid, 140 ℃ and 2 h were chosen for the synthesis of CS-CPDs.

The size and structure of CS-CPDs were characterized by TEM. Fig. 1 a shows that CS-CPDs have a spherical structure with a uniform size of about 2.45 nm and good dispersion. The XRD spectrum of CS-CPDs shows that there was a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 21° (Fig. 1b), corresponding to the C (002) plane, indicating the amorphous carbon structure of CS-CPDs [41,42]. Fig. 1c shows the FT-IR spectra of chitosan and CS-CPDs. After the formation of CS-CPDs, the characteristic absorption peak of chitosan at 3301 cm−1, attributed to the OH and NH stretching vibrations, changed to absorption peak at 3398 cm−1, and the absorption band at 2876 cm−1 belonging to CH decreased [42]. The characteristic CO stretching peak of acetylated amino groups of chitosan at 1655 cm−1 shifted to 1713 cm−1, which was attributed to the CO stretching peak of carboxylic acid groups in CS-CPDs [43]. The absorption peaks of CS-CPDs at 1570, 1389, and 1313 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of NH, CH, and CN, respectively [44]. The other sharp peaks from 989 to 1158 cm−1 of CS-CPDs associated with the pyranose were attributed to the CO and CN stretching and –OH bending vibrations [43]. The FT-IR results indicate that the polymer groups of chitosan are partially retained in CS-CPDs, and finally the CS-CPDs are obtained by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan.

The surface functional groups and elemental composition of CS-CPDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the full spectrum XPS scan of CS-CPDs presents three characteristic peaks at 284.6, 401.5, and 532.5 eV, corresponding to C1s (68.5%), N1s (4.8%), and O1s (26.7%) respectively [45]. The high-resolution (HR) XPS C1s (Fig. 2b) exhibits three characteristic peaks at 284.7, 286.4, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to CC/CN, CO, and CO, respectively. The HR XPS N1s (Fig. 2c) was fitted with two binding energies at 399.9, and 401.6 eV that were assigned to NH and CN. The three characteristic peaks of HR XPS O1s (Fig. 2d) located at 531.6, 532.5, and 533.3 eV were attributed to CO, COH, and COC, respectively [44]. The above results are consistent with the FT-IR results, confirming that polymer groups of chitosan are partially retained in CS-CPDs and finally the CS-CPDs are obtained by hydrothermal carbonization.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured to analyze the optical properties of CS-CPDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CS-CPDs has a weak absorption peak at 210 nm, which is attributed to the π–π* transition of the CC bond [46]. The fluorescence spectra of CS-CPDs show a strong excitation peak at 371 nm, and the emission peak is 510 nm, indicating that CS-CPDs exhibit a large Stokes shift (139 nm). The larger Stokes shift can reduce the interference of excitation light on the emission signal, which is beneficial to suppress the self-absorption of CDs, thereby improving the signal-to-noise ratio and sensitivity, which is of great significance in the fields of fluorescence sensing and optoelectronic devices [47]. The fluorescence quantum yield of as-prepared CS-CPDs was measured to be 17.4% using fluorescein (QY = 92.5%, 0.1 mol/L NaOH) as reference. As shown in Fig. 3b, the excitation dependence of CS-CPDs was studied by changing the excitation wavelength. The position of the fluorescence emission peak remained constant with a series of excitation wavelengths ranging from 330 nm to 440 nm, indicating that the prepared CS-CPDs displayed unique excitation-independence. The excitation-dependence of CS-CPDs is caused by the distribution of different particle sizes and different surface states [48], which indicates that the prepared CS-CPDs have a uniform distribution of particle size and surface states.

To investigate the fluorescence stability and chemical stability of CS-CPDs aqueous solution, the effects of ionic strength, UV-lamp irradiation and pH value on CS-CPDs were investigated in Fig. S2. The results show that the CS-CPDs exhibit excellent optical stability, which predicts broad application prospects in the field of fluorescence sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensitivity and selectivity towards levofloxacin
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe based on CS-CPDs, the following experimental conditions were optimized: (a) incubation time and (b) pH value of the system. The relevant data were given in the Supporting Information (Fig. S3). The optimal experimental conditions were as follows: (a) incubation time of 3 min; (b) pH value of 4.0. Under the optimal conditions, different fluorescence spectra were obtained by varying the concentration of LEV. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CPDs gradually increased with the addition of LEV. The Stern-Volmer equation was used to investigate the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CS-CPDs in the presence of LEV. (Fig. 4b): F/F0 = 0.1732 CLEV + 1.0051, R2 = 0.997, where F0 and F respectively represent the fluorescence intensity of the system before and after LEV addition, CLEV represents the concentration of LEV. The fluorescence signal (F0/F) was linearly proportional to LEV concentration (CLEV ) in the range of 1–35 μmol/L, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.20 μmol/L (3σ/k), where σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank samples, and k represents the slope of the standard curve.

To study the selectivity of CS-CPDs, ofloxacin (OFL), norfloxacin (NOR), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and enrofloxacin (ENO) were selected as competitive substances, which were similar in structure to LEV (Fig. 4c). The experiments were performed by adding CS-CPDs into the above solutions (10 μmol/L), and the changes of the fluorescence signal (F/F0 ) were recorded. Fig. 4d shows that the response signal of CS-CPDs to LEV was higher than that of other structural analogues, and can even distinguish OFL (the dextrorotatory isomer of LEV). Interference tests were also carried out by adding a variety of common cations and anions as interfering substances (10 μmol/L) to the system. As shown in Fig. S4, the selected common ions have no significant interference with the fluorescence signal of CS-CPDs. All results indicate that CS-CPDS has an excellent performance in selectively detecting LEV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn on-off” detection of Cu2+ and the investigation on selectivity
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment, the fluorescence emission intensity of CS-CPDs/LEV was gradually quenched with the increasing of Cu2+ concentration, indicating that CS-CPDs/LEV could be applied to detection of Cu2+ based on fluorescence “turn on-off”. The optimized incubation time for Cu2+ detection was 3 min and LEV concentration was 25 μmol/L (shown in Figs. S5 and S6). Under the optimal conditions, emission spectra were obtained by varying the concentration of Cu2+ (Fig. 5 a). The fluorescence intensity of CS-CPDs/LEV was gradually quenched with the addition of Cu2+. The calibration curve of CS-CPDs/LEV for Cu2+ exhibited a linear relationship in the range of 5–100 μmol/L, and the corresponding LOD was 0.97 μmol/L (Fig. 5b). The limit of quantification (LOQ) can be calculated by the following equation: LOQ = 10σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of the y-intercept of the regression line and k represents the slope of the standard curve. The calculated LOQ was 4.81 μM. The reason for the poor linear performance at the concentration of Cu2+ below 5 μM was the inaccurate detection below the LOQ. To evaluate the selectivity of CS-CPDs/LEV probe towards Cu2+, we explored some common interferences of common ions, sugars and amino acids, and the interference concentration was 100 μmol/L. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, the interference of the above substances except Fe3+ on the detection of Cu2+ was negligible, and the interference caused by Fe3+ can be eliminated by F− [49]. Metal ions with strong coordination will interfere with Cu2+ detection, which should be paid attention to in subsequent practical applications. The above results indicated that CS-CPDs/LEV fluorescent “turn on-off” probe could be applied to selectively detect Cu2+. Table S1 summarized the analytical performance of different fluorescence methods for detecting Cu2+. The proposed method has a wider linear range and comparable detection limits compared to other analytical methods. The LOD is 0.97 μM, which is much lower than the allowable detection limit (∼20 μM) of drinking water set by the EPA. The above results indicate that the fluorescent “turn on-off” probe developed in this paper has broad application prospects in the rapid, sensitive and simple detection of Cu2+ in actual samples.

At present, the fluorescence intensity of most CDs gradually decreases with the addition of the target, and quantitative detection of the target can be achieved based on fluorescence quenching [50]. Interestingly, in this work, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CPDs gradually increased with the addition of LEV. To better understand the possible fluorescence mechanism, UV–vis absorption spectra, FT-IR spectra, Zeta potential and hydration particle size were recorded. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the absorption peak of the mixture at 210 nm was stronger than that of pure CS-CPDs in aqueous solution after LEV was added, indicating the interaction between CS-CPDs and LEV [51]. FT-IR spectra further confirmed the interaction between CS-CPDs and LEV. As shown in Fig. 6b, the absorption peaks of NH stretching vibration at 1570 cm−1 and 3727 cm−1 and the absorption peaks of CN at 1306 cm−1 of CS-CPDs decreased in the FT-IR spectrum of CS-CPDs/LEV mixture, and the characteristic absorptions band for the carboxylic and the pyridone stretch ν(CO) of LEV decreased significantly at 1725 cm−1 and 1621 cm−1, respectively, which further indicated the interaction between CS-CPDs and LEV. As shown in Fig. 6c, the Zeta potential of the system changed from (27.2 ± 0.7) mV to (21.7 ± 0.7) mV after the addition of LEV, which was due to the interaction between LEV and CS-CPDs, and the shielding of the positive charge of the system results in the decrease of Zeta potential [52]. In addition, the hydration particle size of CS-CPDs before and after LEV was measured in Fig. 6d. The hydrated particle size of CS-CPDs/LEV (50.8 nm) was larger than that of CS-CPDs (10.1 nm), indicating that aggregation of CS-CPDs occurred after the addition of LEV. As shown in Fig. S7, TEM images further confirmed the aggregation of CS-CPDs after adding LEV. The aggregation phenomenon restricts the intramolecular motion (vibration and rotation) of CS-CPDs, resulting in a decrease in the non-radiative transitions of CS-CPDs and an increase in fluorescence emission intensity [53]. Therefore, the possible fluorescence enhancement mechanism is AIEE.

The mechanism of fluorescence intensity recovery after adding Cu2+ addition was further explored. With the addition of Cu2+, the absorption peak of CS-CPDs decreased at 210 nm in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 6a), indicating that the interaction between CS-CPDs and LEV was destroyed. LEV belongs to the class of fluoroquinolone drugs, and the 3-carboxyl and 4-carbonyl groups have strong coordination, which provide excellent sites for the coordination of divalent or trivalent metals [54]. The characterization of LEV/Cu2+ complex can be achieved by studying the most typical quinolone coordination type vibrations. As shown in Fig. 6b, the characteristic absorption band of LEV carboxyl and pyridone elongation ν(CO) were located at 1725 cm−1 and 1621 cm−1, respectively. In the CS-CPDs/LEV/Cu2+ complex, the characteristic absorption peak of the carboxyl group disappeared, and ν(CO)p moved to 1576 cm−1. Two characteristic bands were present at 1631 cm−1 and 1394 cm−1, assigned as ν(OCO) asymmetric and symmetric vibrations, respectively, which indicates the deprotonation of the carboxylate moiety and possible coordination with metal ions [55]. The Zeta potential in Fig. 6c changed from (21.7 ± 0.7) mV to (24.9 ± 0.4) mV after adding Cu2+, which also confirmed this result. The Δν = ν(OCO)asym − ν(OCO)sym is used to determine the coordination mode of the quinolone ligands. The Δν value falls at 237 cm−1, indicating the monodentate coordination mode of the carboxylate group [55]. According to the aforementioned data, we could suggest that LEV coordinates with Cu2+ via the pyridone oxygen and a carboxylate oxygen atom, leading to the recovery of fluorescence emission intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of LEV and Cu2+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the fluorescent probe, the method was applied to the detection of LEV and Cu2+ in three environmental water samples (South Lake water, Yan Lake water and Tap water). No LEV was detected in these environmental water samples. To further confirm the feasibility of the developed fluorescent probe, a standard additive recovery experiment was carried out, and the results were summarized in Table 1 . Recoveries of LEV in environmental water samples were in the range of 91.3–99.6%, and the RSDs were less than 2.3% (n = 3). For comparation purpose, the analytical performance of different LEV fluorescence detection methods was summarized in Table S2. Compared with the previous methods, this method has a wide linear range and good detection limit, and provides satisfactory recovery in the detection of LEV in environment water samples. The analytical performance of the prepared CS-CPDs in real samples such as serum and urine can then be explored to expand the practical application potential of CS-CPDs.

ICP-OES was used to determine Cu2+ concentrations in three environmental water samples, which were very low and below the detection limit. Fluorescence detection was performed on a series of spiked water samples, and the results were summarized in Table 2 . Recoveries of Cu2+ detection in environmental water samples were in the range of 97.7–113.4%, with RSDs less than 2.0% (n = 3). The results reveal that the developed CS-CPDs fluorescent “turn on-off” probe shows high accuracy and applicability in environmental water samples analysis.

Based on the above analysis, the prepared CS-CPDs achieved rapid and sensitive detection of LEV and Cu2+ in environmental water samples based on fluorescence “turn on-off”. However, the surface functional groups of CS-CPDs tend to cross-link to form complexes that interfere with their fluorescence properties after long-term storage, so CS-CPDs need to be re-prepared before detection. Moreover, transition metal ions with strong coordination have interference in the detection process of Cu2+, which needs to be paid attention to in the actual detection.

In summary, a simple, inexpensive and environmentally-friendly route to prepare biomass carbonized polymer dots (CS-CPDs) was developed by the one-step microwave-assisted method using biomass polymer chitosan and maleic acid as the precursors. The as-prepared CS-CPDs have an amorphous carbon structure with narrow size distribution and exhibited excellent optical stability and large Stokes shift (139 nm), with a fluorescence quantum yield of 17.4%. Remarkably, based on AIEE and coordination, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CPDs was enhanced with the addition of LEV and quickly recovered with the continuous addition of Cu2+. Therefore, a novel fluorescent “turn on-off” probe based on CS-CPDs was developed for continuous detection of LEV and Cu2+, which has been successfully used for the detection of LEV and Cu2+ in environmental water samples, with excellent resistance to interference and satisfactory detection results. According to the above results, the fluorescent “turn on-off” probe developed in this paper has extremely high practical application potential in the continuous detection of LEV and Cu2+ in environmental water samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 201804049].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2022.07.018.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
